perturbation.original_text: 3. The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those em ployees, consultants and third party companies whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order and whic h are legally or contractually obligated to secrecy .
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those em ployees, consultants and third party companies whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order and whic h are commercially obligated to secrecy.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'legally or contractually obligated to secrecy' with 'commercially obligated to secrecy' weakens the legal enforceability of the confidentiality. Commercial obligation is a vague term and is not recognized as a legal substitute for legally binding confidentiality agreements, leading to a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the strength of legal protection for trade secrets and confidential information, as the Trade Secrets Act relies on clear legal obligations, not just commercial expectations. The term 'commercially obligated' does not have the same weight as 'legally or contractually obligated' in a court of law, thereby potentially failing to meet the standards required for trade secret protection under federal law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'legally or contractually obligated to secrecy' to 'commercially obligated to secrecy' weakens the legal enforceability of confidentiality. The Law Explanation indicates that the Trade Secrets Act relies on clear legal obligations, not just commercial expectations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation (18 U.S. Code § 1836), it underscores the importance of trade secret protection. Changing the obligation to 'commercially obligated' introduces ambiguity and makes it less clear that the parties are bound by legally enforceable confidentiality agreements, which could undermine the protection of trade secrets if misappropriation occurs. The original text explicitly stated the involved parties were legally bound, whereas the perturbed text replaces that with a weaker, undefined commercial obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is tacitly extended by one year if it is not terminated three months before its expiration.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This agreement becomes effective with the signat ures of both parties and is valid for three years. It is automatically extended by one year if it is not terminated three months before its expiration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'tacitly extended' to 'automatically extended' can create ambiguity. While seemingly minor, 'tacitly' implies implied consent, whereas 'automatically' implies no consent is needed. This can conflict with contract law principles requiring mutual agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under German Civil Code (BGB), contract extensions, even if pre-agreed, generally require some form of affirmation or at least the absence of explicit objection within a reasonable timeframe. 'Automatically extended' suggests an indefinite extension without the possibility of objection, conflicting with the principle of mutual consent inherent in German contract law. § 145 ff. BGB require a declaration of intent for contract establishment, which tacit consent acknowledges, unlike automatic renewal.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'tacitly extended' to 'automatically extended' contradicts the principle of mutual consent inherent in contract law, as explained in the Law Explanation. While the scraped law snippets do not directly address tacit vs automatic consent, the law explanation argues that German Civil Code (BGB) § 145 ff. requires a declaration of intent for contract establishment, something that automatic renewal circumvents. The changed text implies an extension without requiring any form of affirmation or objection within a reasonable timeframe, which undermines the need for mutual agreement and creates an unenforceable obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 11. The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding the United Nations Conventi on on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is the head office of the company Helukabel in Hemmingen.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding any international treaty or law. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the specific exclusion of the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) with a blanket exclusion of 'any international treaty or law' and changing the venue to 'any court of competent jurisdiction' broadens the scope of exclusion and introduces uncertainty. This contradicts the standard practice of specifying applicable laws and venues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG)
perturbation.law_explanation: The alteration attempts to exclude all international treaties which is not possible under most legal frameworks. By removing the specification and moving venue to any competent jurisdiction, parties are not able to predict in advance what rules will apply and the German courts might be bypassed entirely, which is usually against the freedom of choice.
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the exclusion of laws and changes the venue for disputes. The original text specifies German law, excluding CISG, and sets the venue at Helukabel's head office. The changed text excludes 'any international treaty or law' and allows 'any court of competent jurisdiction'. The Perturbation Explanation states this 'contradicts the standard practice of specifying applicable laws and venues' and introduces uncertainty. The Law Explanation adds that excluding all international treaties is not possible and parties cannot predict which rules apply. The absence of supporting scraped snippets does not negate the legal contradiction introduced by the broad exclusion and change of venue.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Every contract party can disclose confidential i nformation to the other contract party orally or in writing. If the disclosure is in writing, the disclosing party will mark the confidential documents with the additiona l terms “Confidential” or “Liable to Secrecy” so that it is apparent to the other party which information is e specially in need to protection.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Every contract party can disclose confidential i nformation to the other contract party orally or in writing. If the disclosure is in writing, the disclosing party may mark the confidential documents with the terms “Confidential” or “Liable to Secrecy”.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will mark' to 'may mark' changes the obligation of marking confidential documents from mandatory to optional. This creates ambiguity and weakens the protection of confidential information, especially under trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the explicit obligation to identify and mark confidential information, creating ambiguity. This weakens the protection afforded under German unfair competition law (UWG § 4 No. 3), which safeguards business and trade secrets against misappropriation. By making marking optional, it becomes more difficult to prove the intent to protect confidentiality, thereby hindering the enforcement of secrecy obligations.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates marking confidential documents with specific terms ('will mark'), while the changed text makes this marking optional ('may mark'). This weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this change hinders the enforcement of secrecy obligations under German unfair competition law (UWG § 4 No. 3). Although the scraped law snippets do not directly address marking requirements, the change from 'will' to 'may' directly reduces the legally expected standard of protection. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where contract language deviates from legally recognized protection levels, making the contract less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 9. At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, provided that this is technically feasible and does not vio late the retention obligations. As an alternative to that, the receivi ng party can also ensure in writing the destruction of the received documentation if that is technically feasible.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a verbal request, regardless of retention obligations. As an alternative to that, the receivi ng party can also ensure in writing the destruction of the received documentation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written request' to 'verbal request' and removing the 'technically feasible and does not violate the retention obligations' clause undermines the standard of evidence required for contract enforcement and contradicts legally mandated retention policies. Requesting verbal returns rather than written returns introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification implies immediate return irrespective of retention obligations, conflicting with GDPR requirements for data retention. Specifically, Article 17, while outlining the 'right to be forgotten,' acknowledges that data must be retained when legally required. Obligating immediate return irrespective of such obligations (e.g., tax law) would violate GDPR and related national implementations, creating legal conflict. Verbal requests provide no audit trail and could lead to destruction of documents that need to be retained for compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a legal contradiction. The original text requires a 'written request' for returning confidential information, while the changed text accepts a 'verbal request'. The change also removes the clause regarding retention obligations. The Law Explanation indicates this conflicts with GDPR requirements for data retention, as verbal requests offer no audit trail and could lead to the destruction of documents that need to be retained for compliance. Article 17 of GDPR, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, acknowledges that data must be retained when legally required. Obligating immediate return irrespective of such obligations would violate GDPR. The modified contract's acceptance of verbal requests and removal of retention obligation consideration directly contradicts the legal requirement for data retention and auditability, making the contract non-compliant.
